subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
Global components shown in the provided rulebook excerpt: 1 game board, 1 quick-reference guide, 5 player mats, 42 combat cards, 23 objective cards, 28 encounter cards, 12 Factory cards, 80 resource tokens (20 each of food, metal, wood, and oil), 2 power dials, 80 cardboard coins, 5 Riverwalk cards, 12 multiplier tokens, 12 encounter tokens, 5 quick-start cards, and 6 structure bonus tiles. 

Faction components per faction (5 factions shown: Nordic, Saxony, Polania, Crimea, Rusviet): 1 faction mat, 1 action token, 1 popularity token, 1 power token, 6 star tokens, 4 structure tokens, 4 recruit tokens, 4 mech miniatures, 1 character miniature, 8 workers, and 6 technology cubes. 

Total across all five factions from the provided matrix: 5 faction mats, 5 action tokens, 5 popularity tokens, 5 power tokens, 30 stars, 20 structures, 20 recruits, 20 mechs, 5 characters, 40 workers, and 30 technology cubes.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: 1) Place the game board centrally so all tracks are visible (Triumph, Popularity, Power). 2) Place one encounter token on each territory marked with encounter symbol (excerpt indicates 11 total on board setup panel; component list includes 12 encounter tokens in box). 3) Place resource tokens, coins, and multiplier tokens in a shared supply area beside the board. 4) Shuffle combat cards and place deck on board; if deck empties, reshuffle discard to form new deck; if both empty, no cards are drawn. 5) Shuffle encounter cards and objective cards into separate decks and place on board. 6) Randomly choose one structure bonus tile and place it face up at the bottom of the Popularity Track. 7) Shuffle Factory cards and place face-down a number equal to players + 1 on the Factory spaces; return unused Factory cards to box unseen. 8) Randomly deal each player one Faction Mat and one Player Mat; players sit near matching home bases (clockwise faction order shown in setup example: Nordic, Rusviet, Crimea, Saxony, Polania).

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
Set up each player’s faction and player mat exactly as printed on those mats:

Faction mat setup: 
- Place power token on indicated starting value on Power Track.
- Draw indicated number of combat cards (count is public, card faces hidden).
- Gain indicated number of coins.

Player mat setup:
- Place popularity token on indicated starting value on Popularity Track.
- Draw indicated number of objective cards (hidden).
- Place 6 technology cubes on green top-row action spaces containing black-square marks.
- Place 4 structures on matching bottom-row structure boxes.
- Place 4 recruit tokens on circular recruit spaces.
- Place remaining 6 workers on Produce action rectangles.
- Place action token next to player mat for turn selection.

Board presence setup:
- Place character miniature on your faction home base.
- Place 1 worker on each territory connected by land to your home base (2 workers total on map at start).
- Place your 4 mechs on mech spaces of faction mat and 6 stars on faction mat’s star area/triumph reserve.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: After all players complete setup, determine first player by Player Mat number: the player with the lowest-numbered player mat starts. Play proceeds clockwise. Each player begins with a unique faction/player-mat combination, private objective card(s), private combat cards, and public starting track positions for popularity/power. The game begins immediately with turn-by-turn action selection from each player’s player mat.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
On your turn, resolve in this order: 
1) Choose one action section (column) on your Player Mat. You may not choose the same section selected on your previous turn. 
2) Resolve the top-row action of that chosen section (if possible/applicable). 
3) Resolve the bottom-row action of that same section (if you can and want to pay its cost). Top and bottom are always tied to the same column. 
4) End your turn; play passes clockwise. 

At any time you satisfy a star condition, place a star immediately on the Triumph Track space for that achievement type (subject to per-type limits, such as combat stars). 

The game ends immediately when any player places their 6th star. Then proceed to final scoring.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Scythe actions come from your Player Mat’s paired top/bottom action system. The provided excerpt and quick reference identify these major actions and constraints: 

TOP-ROW ACTION TYPES 
- Move: Move eligible units according to movement rules; units may transport any number of resource tokens. Mechs can also transport workers; mechs cannot transport characters. Some effects allow one unit to move twice. 
- Produce: Generate resources/workers on territories where you have workers, following production limits and costs as printed on your mat. 
- Trade: Gain selected resources and/or popularity according to your specific player mat layout. 
- Bolster: Gain power and/or combat cards depending on selected bolster option and mat state. 

BOTTOM-ROW ACTION TYPES 
- Upgrade (usually costs oil): Move one technology cube from a top-row action slot to a bottom-row action cost slot, improving one top action and reducing one bottom action cost. 
- Deploy (usually costs metal): Place one mech from faction mat to a territory you control where you have a worker (per deploy rules). 
- Build (usually costs wood): Place one structure from your mat onto an eligible territory you control with your worker present. 
- Enlist (usually costs food): Remove one recruit token from your player mat and gain associated enlist benefit/trigger. 

ACTION RESTRICTIONS AND PAYMENT RULES 
- You can only choose one action section per turn, and not the same as last turn. 
- You may take both top and bottom actions in that section in the listed order, but bottom actions require payment. 
- Resources used for payment must come from territories you currently control. 
- You can spend resources from any territories you control, not just the territory where an effect happens. 
- Spent resources return to supply. 

RESOURCE DEFINITIONS 
- Resources are only wood, food, metal, and oil. Workers are not resources. 
- Resources remain on territories when produced until moved/spent/lost with control changes. 
- There is no hard cap on resource token quantity (use multiplier tokens if needed). 

UNIT CAPABILITIES (from provided important concepts pages) 
- Characters: can move, transport resources, engage in combat, trigger encounters, and can gain one Factory card once per game by ending movement on Factory. 
- Mechs: can move, transport resources, engage in combat, and can transport workers. 
- Workers: can move and transport resources; they produce resources/workers and are required for deploy/build presence conditions; they do not participate in combat.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Territory and control system: A territory is one board hex of type farm, forest, mountain, tundra, village, lake, factory, or home base zone. You control a territory if you have at least one unit there (character, mech, or worker), OR if you have a structure there with no enemy units present. Only one player controls a territory at a time. 

Home base rule: Home bases are not territories. By default you cannot move units, deploy mechs, or build structures onto any home base (including your own), except through specific rule exceptions/effects. 

Resource exposure: Because resources stay on the map, map control is economic control. Losing control of a territory can deny spending access to all resources there. 

Plastic vs wood cue: plastic pieces (characters and mechs) are combat-capable units; wood pieces (workers, resources, structures) are not combat participants. 

Encounter system: Encounter tokens on marked territories can be triggered by character movement, granting choice-based card effects from encounter deck. 

Factory system: Factory is a unique territory and objective focus. A character ending movement there can gain one Factory card once per game. The quick reference excerpt also notes Factory territory has special scoring treatment (counts as three territories in that reference). 

Objective system: Objective cards are private goals. At any time during your turn when you complete an objective, you may place a star on objective achievement space and discard/reveal as required. 

Stars and achievements: You can earn stars by major milestones listed in source text: all 6 upgrades, all 4 mechs, all 4 structures, all 4 recruits, all 8 workers on board, reveal 1 completed objective, win combat (up to 2 stars), reach 18 popularity, and reach 16 power. Each achievement generally awards at most one star unless explicitly capped differently (combat cap called out). 

Combat overview: Combat happens when opposing combat units share territory. Players commit power (via dial) and optionally combat cards, reveal totals, and higher total wins; loser retreats to home base. Winning combat may grant combat star up to cap. Power spent in combat is not recovered automatically, creating long-term deterrence and tempo decisions.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
End trigger: the game ends immediately when a player places their 6th star on the Triumph Track. 

Final scoring uses coins (fortune), not victory points: 
1) Coins for each star token placed. 
2) Coins for each territory controlled. 
3) Coins for each set of every 2 resources controlled. 

Popularity multiplier: The number of coins gained from those three categories depends on your position on the Popularity Track. Higher popularity yields more coins per scored item. 

Structure bonus: Gain additional coins from the selected structure bonus tile based on where your structures were built and the bonus condition in play. 

Total winner: player with the most total coins wins. 

Tie-breakers (in order, from provided quick reference excerpt): most territories controlled, then most resources controlled, then most stars placed.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Build an action loop, not a pile of random gains. Because you can’t repeat the same action section in consecutive turns, your first objective is to create a two- or three-column cycle that consistently feeds costs for bottom-row actions. 
2) Deploy early workers and secure safe production zones. Workers are your production base and your deploy/build enablers; weak worker development delays every major star path. 
3) Prioritize one early bottom-row advancement path (often Upgrade or Deploy) to accelerate tempo. Upgrades reduce future action friction and can snowball value over many turns. 
4) Track popularity from turn 1. Endgame coin conversion is heavily popularity-dependent, so an economy that ignores popularity often “looks strong” midgame but loses in final scoring. 
5) Expand with purpose. Territory control matters twice (direct category scoring and resource access). Early expansion should either improve production efficiency, deny key opponents, or set up encounter/factory opportunities.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Treating workers like expendable combat units. Workers do not fight, and careless worker positioning can cause large tempo and control losses. 
2) Overinvesting in combat stars beyond cap logic. The game only awards up to two combat stars, so repeated fights after that often burn power/cards for low return unless they secure critical territory timing. 
3) Floating huge resource piles on exposed front lines. On-map resources are vulnerable through control shifts; unbanked resources can become opponent leverage. 
4) Ignoring player-mat sequencing rule (no same section twice) in planning. New players often choose a “great” action now that strands them into weak next-turn options. 
5) Delaying objective awareness. Objectives are private and can become easy stars if planned around early; forgetting them until late can cost a free achievement. 
6) Rushing to sixth star without checking coin conversion quality. Ending the game while low on popularity/territory/resources can hand victory to someone with fewer stars but better scoring tiers.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Star race speed vs score quality: You can accelerate toward six stars quickly, but if popularity tier and territory/resource control are weak, you might trigger an endgame you cannot win. Deciding when to push the trigger is one of Scythe’s defining trade-offs. 

Engine growth vs board pressure: Investing in upgrades/enlist/production compounding yields stronger later turns, but if you surrender map tempo, opponents may claim encounters, factory leverage, and structure-bonus lanes uncontested. 

Power deterrence vs power spending: High visible power discourages attacks and protects your economy; however, holding power too long can mean missed tactical combats and stars. Choosing when to convert threat into actual combat outcomes is a pivotal judgment call each game.

metadata.notes: Primary source used: provided Scythe rulebook excerpt and quick reference text.
